regulator: DFSA (Dubai Financial Services Authority -- DIFC only) / FSRA (Financial Services Regulatory Authority -- ADGM only) / CMA (Capital Markets Authority -- federal, UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM; legal successor to the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) since 1 January 2026) -- VARA (Emirate of Dubai, including its free zones, excluding the DIFC) has no dedicated fund/collective-investment-scheme licensing regime and is not a primary regulator for this pillar (see standing_summary)
instruments: DFSA Rulebook, General Module (GEN), Chapter 3A (Crypto Tokens) -- under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC; current version confirmed by direct fetch of dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/entiresection/25729 as VER71/01-26, itself amended by DFSA instrument RMI423/2025 dated 15 December 2025 and in force from January 2026 (corroborated by secondary legal-alert reporting -- Ocorian, Banks Legal, Simmons & Simmons -- as 12 January 2026, the same date independently confirmed for this jurisdiction's Exchanges (VATP) pillar); GEN Rule 3A.1.2(1) provides that 'a Fund invests in a Crypto Token if: (a) any of its property includes the Crypto Token' or it has derivative exposure, tracks an index containing the token, or invests in another Fund or entity with such exposure, as directly quoted from the fetched rule text; GEN Rule 3A.1.2(2) disregards indirect exposure through another Fund or entity where 'the total aggregate exposure of that other Fund or entity to all Crypto Tokens does not exceed 5%' of that other Fund or entity's gross asset value, or where the exposure results solely from tracking an index that does not itself specifically track the Crypto Token in question -- both quoted from the directly fetched rule text; GEN Rule 3A.2.1(1)(d) prohibits an Authorised Firm from carrying on listed activities, including '(i) Managing a Collective Investment Fund' and acting as trustee or making financial promotions, 'in relation to a Fund that invests in the Crypto Token' unless the DFSA's suitability conditions are satisfied, with parallel prohibitions for Funds investing in a Privacy Token (GEN Rule 3A.2.2) and an Algorithmic Token (GEN Rule 3A.2.3) -- all directly quoted/confirmed from the fetched rule text; accompanying Guidance GEN 3A.2.1A confirms this prohibition 'applies to indirect investment in Crypto Tokens such as by the use of Feeder Funds or Exchange Traded Funds', and states that where a Fund's existing Crypto Token exposure is later assessed as no longer suitable, 'the DFSA expects that the Fund will ... divest or otherwise eliminate its exposure to the Crypto Token' -- both directly quoted from the fetched guidance text

DFSA Rulebook, Collective Investment Rules (CIR) -- under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC; current version confirmed by direct fetch of dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/collective-investment-rules-cir-ver4001-26 as VER40/01-26, in force from January 2026; the fetched table of contents confirms a dedicated section, 'CIR 12A Requirements Specific to Qualified Investor Funds', within Part 5 of the module -- the section this research understands (per corroborating secondary legal-alert reporting on the same January 2026 rule package, not independently re-fetched article-by-article in this pass) to carry the Fund-specific Crypto Token exposure provisions cross-referenced from GEN 3A.1.2/3A.2.1 above; the glossary term 'Qualified Investor Fund' is confirmed by direct fetch of dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/qualified-investor-fund as defined by cross-reference to 'Article 16(5) of the Collective Investment Law 2010' (DFSA instrument RM143/2014, effective 21 August 2014) -- the fetched glossary entry itself did not display the Crypto Token exposure text, which sits in CIR 12A rather than the glossary

FSRA Fund Rules (FUNDS Rulebook) and the Guidance -- Regulation of Virtual Asset Activities in ADGM, as amended -- under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM; FSRA's own 10 June 2025 announcement of amendments to its digital-asset regulatory framework, fetched directly from adgm.com/media/announcements/adgm-fsra-implements-amendments-to-its-digital-asset-regulatory-framework, confirms in force with immediate effect changes including that 'the amendments expand the scope of investments in which Venture Capital Funds may invest' to include Virtual Assets, 'either directly or through an underlying master fund' -- directly quoted from the fetched announcement text; the same announcement confirms the amendments also permit banks to carry on virtual-asset custody and formalise prohibitions on privacy tokens and algorithmic stablecoins/similar tokens, though it does not itself set out fund-type-by-fund-type detail beyond the Venture Capital Fund statement quoted above; secondary legal-alert reporting (King & Spalding; ADGM-focused practitioner commentary) attributes this change to FUNDS Rule 4.1.6 and describes it as extending to Qualified Investor Funds as well as Venture Capital Funds, but this session's repeated direct-fetch attempts against the ADGM/Thomson Reuters-hosted rulebook pages for the FUNDS module (en.adgm.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/funds-416 and related entiresection pages) returned HTTP 403 responses, so the specific rule number and its exact wording (including whether Qualified Investor Funds are covered, and any percentage-based eligibility conditions) are not independently primary-verified in this pass -- flagged in open_items

ADGM's own digital-assets overview page (adgm.com/setting-up/digital-assets, fetched directly) states that 'Collective Investment Funds investing in Digital Assets, are regulated as Derivatives and Units in a Fund by the FSRA', and that 'Market operators and intermediaries dealing in such Derivatives and Funds are required to be licensed by the FSRA under the applicable financial services regulations' -- directly quoted from the fetched page; this confirms, at the level of ADGM's own public-facing regulatory description, that a fund investing in digital assets is treated within the existing Fund Rules/FSMR framework rather than as a separate crypto-specific fund product category, consistent with the targeted Venture-Capital-Fund-specific amendment described above rather than a wholesale new fund regime

CMA's federal Investment Funds regulatory category -- under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM); the CMA's own regulations portal (uaecma.gov.ae/en/regulations/regulations-listing, fetched directly) displays a regulation category described as 'the establishment and management of investment funds', and the CMA's own homepage/open-data listings (uaecma.gov.ae, fetched directly) reference 'Authorized and Registered Investment Funds' as an existing open-data category and 'Amendment to a local investment fund offering document' as an existing licensed service -- confirming the CMA (as SCA's federal successor since 1 January 2026, per Federal Decree-Law No. 32 of 2025, independently fetched and cited in full in this jurisdiction's Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar) operates a general federal investment-funds regulatory regime; this research did not, however, locate or fetch any CMA-issued instrument that creates a crypto-asset-specific or virtual-asset-specific investment fund or exchange-traded-product category, or that amends the federal investment-funds regime to add or restrict virtual-asset holdings -- see open_items

CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 (Virtual Assets Framework) -- under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM); per secondary legal-alert reporting (Horiz Law & Co, Clyde & Co, Mielo Group) corroborated in general terms by a CMA news item on uaecma.gov.ae, this Decision licenses eight virtual-asset activities including portfolio management and investment advice, and reportedly sets a minimum capital requirement of AED 1,000,000 for fund managers/discretionary virtual-asset portfolio managers; these are manager-level licensing requirements, not a fund/collective-investment-scheme product regime, and are tracked in full in this jurisdiction's Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar rather than restated here; this research found no provision within the reporting on Decision No. 4/R.M/2026, nor on the CMA's own domain, establishing a fund or ETF product category specific to virtual assets

VARA rulebook structure -- under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC); VARA's own rulebook index (rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/rulebooks, fetched directly) lists eight VA Activity and other rulebooks -- Advisory Services, Broker-Dealer Services, Custody Services, Exchange Services, Lending and Borrowing Services, VA Management and Investment Services, VA Transfer and Settlement Services, and Virtual Asset Issuance -- with no separate 'Fund' or 'Collective Investment Scheme' rulebook or licensed activity among them; the VA Management and Investment Services Rulebook (rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/va-management-and-investment-services-rulebook, fetched directly, effective 19 June 2025) governs discretionary portfolio/investment management of a client's virtual assets but is a manager-licensing regime, not a fund-product regime, and is tracked in full in this jurisdiction's Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar; this research found no VARA rulebook, guidance document, or Dubai legislation (dlp.dubai.gov.ae) establishing a fund or exchange-traded-product licensing category for virtual assets
standing_summary: This pillar is genuinely thin across the UAE's parallel regulatory slices, and what does exist sits almost entirely with two of the five regulators -- DFSA and FSRA -- with the CMA's federal regime and VARA's Dubai (ex-DIFC) regime each contributing, at most, general fund-regulation infrastructure that has not been shown, on the primary sources this research could fetch, to address virtual-asset-specific fund or ETF products. Under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC, this research directly fetched the DFSA Rulebook's General Module Chapter 3A (Crypto Tokens), current version VER71/01-26, itself amended by DFSA instrument RMI423/2025 of 15 December 2025 and in force from January 2026 (12 January 2026, per corroborating secondary reporting and this jurisdiction's own directly-confirmed Exchanges (VATP) pillar finding). GEN Rule 3A.1.2(1) defines when 'a Fund invests in a Crypto Token' -- direct holding, derivative exposure, index-tracking, or exposure via investment in another Fund or entity -- and GEN Rule 3A.1.2(2) disregards indirect exposure through another Fund or entity where 'the total aggregate exposure of that other Fund or entity to all Crypto Tokens does not exceed 5%' of its gross asset value, or where exposure arises solely from tracking a non-crypto-specific index; both provisions were quoted directly from the fetched rule text. GEN Rule 3A.2.1(1)(d) then prohibits an Authorised Firm from 'Managing a Collective Investment Fund', acting as trustee, or making financial promotions 'in relation to a Fund that invests in the Crypto Token' unless the DFSA's token-suitability conditions are met, with parallel prohibitions in GEN 3A.2.2 and 3A.2.3 for Funds investing in Privacy Tokens and Algorithmic Tokens respectively; accompanying Guidance GEN 3A.2.1A, also directly fetched, confirms these prohibitions extend to indirect exposure 'such as by the use of Feeder Funds or Exchange Traded Funds' and states the DFSA's expectation that a Fund whose Crypto Token exposure is later assessed unsuitable will 'divest or otherwise eliminate' that exposure. The DFSA's Collective Investment Rules (CIR) module, also directly fetched (current version VER40/01-26, in force January 2026), confirms a dedicated section -- 'CIR 12A Requirements Specific to Qualified Investor Funds' -- within Part 5, which this research understands from corroborating secondary legal-alert reporting (not independently re-fetched article-by-article in this pass) to carry the QIF-specific detail of the Crypto Token exposure regime just described, including secondary reports that a prior 30% aggregate exposure limit for Qualified Investor Funds and a 10% limit for Domestic Qualified Investor Funds investing in unrecognised tokens have been removed as part of the January 2026 update, replaced by the suitability-and-disclosure approach in GEN 3A described above; that specific removed-limit figure is reported only in secondary sources in this pass and is flagged in open_items rather than asserted as independently primary-verified. Under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM, FSRA's own 10 June 2025 announcement of amendments to its digital-asset regulatory framework -- fetched directly from adgm.com -- states plainly that 'the amendments expand the scope of investments in which Venture Capital Funds may invest' to include Virtual Assets, 'either directly or through an underlying master fund', in force with immediate effect from that date; ADGM's own digital-assets overview page, also fetched directly, separately confirms that 'Collective Investment Funds investing in Digital Assets are regulated as Derivatives and Units in a Fund by the FSRA' and that market operators and intermediaries dealing in such funds require FSRA licensing under the applicable financial services regulations -- i.e., FSRA folds virtual-asset fund exposure into its existing Fund Rules (FUNDS Rulebook) and Financial Services and Markets Regulations framework via a targeted amendment, rather than creating a wholly separate fund-product category. Secondary legal-alert reporting (King & Spalding and other ADGM-focused commentary) attributes the Venture Capital Fund change to FUNDS Rule 4.1.6 and describes an accompanying widening for Qualified Investor Funds; this session's repeated attempts to directly fetch the FUNDS Rulebook's own text from the ADGM/Thomson Reuters rulebook host returned HTTP 403 responses each time, so the exact rule number, its precise wording, and whether Qualified Investor Funds are covered on the same or different terms as Venture Capital Funds are not independently primary-verified here and are flagged in open_items. Under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM), the CMA -- SCA's federal legal successor since 1 January 2026 under Federal Decree-Law No. 32 of 2025, fetched and cited in full in this jurisdiction's Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar -- maintains a general federal investment-funds regulatory category, confirmed by direct fetch of the CMA's own regulations portal (a category described as 'the establishment and management of investment funds') and of open-data/service listings on the CMA's own site referencing 'Authorized and Registered Investment Funds' and fund-offering-document amendment services. This research did not, however, locate or fetch any CMA-issued instrument creating a virtual-asset-specific fund or exchange-traded-product category, or amending the federal investment-funds regime to address virtual-asset holdings. CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 (the federal Virtual Assets Framework, tracked in full in the Exchanges (VATP) and Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillars), per secondary reporting corroborated in general terms by a CMA news item on uaecma.gov.ae, licenses portfolio management and investment-advice activity relating to virtual assets with a reported AED 1,000,000 minimum capital requirement for fund managers/discretionary virtual-asset portfolio managers -- but this is manager-level activity licensing, not a fund or ETF product regime, and this research found no CMA fund-product provision to report in this pillar. Finally, VARA -- the regulator for the Emirate of Dubai, including its free zones, excluding the DIFC -- has no dedicated fund or collective-investment-scheme licensing category at all: VARA's own rulebook index, directly fetched, lists eight VA Activity and other rulebooks (Advisory, Broker-Dealer, Custody, Exchange, Lending and Borrowing, VA Management and Investment Services, VA Transfer and Settlement, and Virtual Asset Issuance Services), none of which is a Fund or Collective Investment Scheme rulebook; the closest analogue, the VA Management and Investment Services Rulebook, licenses discretionary portfolio/investment managers rather than fund vehicles and is tracked in full in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar. No VARA rulebook, guidance document, or Dubai legislation instrument establishing a fund or exchange-traded-product licensing category for virtual assets was located in this research pass. In sum: this pillar has a real, currently in-force, directly-verified regime only under DFSA (DIFC) and, to a narrower extent, FSRA (ADGM); the CMA's federal layer and VARA's Dubai (ex-DIFC) layer currently show general fund-regulation or manager-licensing infrastructure but no confirmed virtual-asset-specific fund/ETF product rule, and this card says so plainly rather than inferring or padding a claim neither regulator's own text supports.

key_links.label: DFSA Rulebook: GEN 3A -- Crypto Tokens (definitions, fund-exposure rules, prohibitions; VER71/01-26)
DFSA Rulebook: Collective Investment Rules (CIR) -- VER40/01-26, incl. CIR 12A Requirements Specific to Qualified Investor Funds
DFSA Rulebook: Glossary -- Qualified Investor Fund (cross-referencing Collective Investment Law 2010, Art. 16(5))
DFSA: Crypto Token Regulation overview page
ADGM: FSRA implements amendments to its Digital Asset Regulatory Framework (10 June 2025) -- Venture Capital Fund virtual-asset investment scope
ADGM: Digital Assets setting-up overview -- Collective Investment Funds investing in Digital Assets regulated as Units in a Fund by the FSRA
CMA: regulations listing, incl. 'the establishment and management of investment funds' category
CMA: official homepage (open-data Authorized and Registered Investment Funds listing; investment-fund service listings)
VARA Rulebook: Rulebooks index (no dedicated Fund/Collective Investment Scheme rulebook)
VARA Rulebook: VA Management and Investment Services Rulebook (manager licensing, not a fund-product regime)

open_items: Secondary legal-alert sources (Ocorian, Banks Legal, Simmons & Simmons) report that the January 2026 DFSA update removed a prior 30% aggregate Crypto Token exposure limit for Qualified Investor Funds and a 10% limit for Domestic Qualified Investor Funds investing in unrecognised tokens. This session directly fetched and confirmed the current GEN 3A.1.2/3A.2.1 suitability-based regime and the existence of CIR 12A (Requirements Specific to Qualified Investor Funds), but did not independently fetch and quote either the prior (pre-January-2026) CIR/GEN percentage-limit text or the CIR 12A provision text itself article-by-article. The specific 30%/10% removed-limit figures should be treated as reported-but-not-independently-primary-verified until CIR 12A's full text or the DFSA's own consultation/final-rules documents (CP168 and its outcome) are directly fetched.

Secondary legal-alert reporting (King & Spalding and other ADGM-focused commentary) attributes FSRA's 10 June 2025 Venture Capital Fund virtual-asset amendment to FUNDS Rule 4.1.6, and describes a parallel widening of eligible virtual-asset holdings for Qualified Investor Funds. This session's repeated direct-fetch attempts against the ADGM/Thomson Reuters-hosted FUNDS Rulebook pages (en.adgm.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/funds-416 and related entiresection pages) all returned HTTP 403 responses, so the specific rule number, its exact wording, and the Qualified Investor Fund treatment are not independently primary-verified in this pass. FSRA's own 10 June 2025 announcement (fetched directly from adgm.com) confirms only the Venture Capital Fund statement quoted in this card's standing_summary. Should be re-fetched once the FUNDS Rulebook pages are accessible, or via the underlying Consultation Paper No. 11 of 2024 PDF (assets.adgm.com), which also returned HTTP 403 to this session's fetch attempts.

No CMA-issued instrument creating a virtual-asset-specific investment fund or exchange-traded-product category was located. The CMA's own regulations-listing page (uaecma.gov.ae/en/regulations/regulations-listing) is a client-rendered search interface that this session's fetch tooling could not resolve to underlying decision text (the same limitation recorded in this jurisdiction's Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar for CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026). It remains possible that a CMA instrument on this topic exists but was not surfaced by the searches and fetch attempts made in this pass; this pillar should be re-checked directly against the CMA's regulations portal once a stable, server-rendered decision-text URL is identified, and against CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026's own text for any fund-adjacent provisions beyond the manager-licensing activities already tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar.

No VARA rulebook, guidance document, or Dubai Legislation (dlp.dubai.gov.ae) instrument establishing a fund or collective-investment-scheme licensing category for virtual assets was located in this research pass; this is recorded as an honest negative finding (VARA's own rulebook index, directly fetched, shows no such rulebook among its eight VA Activity and other rulebooks) rather than an assumption that no such regime could ever exist. Should be re-checked at the next research pass and against dlp.dubai.gov.ae directly, since Dubai-level (non-VARA) legislation was not separately searched in this pass.

This pillar tracks only fund/ETF product-level regulation -- i.e., rules governing what a Fund may hold and how it may be structured and marketed. Manager-level licensing for entities that manage funds or portfolios containing virtual assets (VARA's VA Management and Investment Services Rulebook; DFSA's and FSRA's asset-management Financial Services categories; CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026's portfolio-management and investment-advice activities) is tracked in full in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar and not duplicated here.

This pillar does not cover the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE); the CBUAE has no confirmed fund/ETF product regime and the pillar brief allocates CBUAE-owned instruments (Payment Token Services Regulation, banking-side rules, the Digital Dirham programme) to the Banking & Money and Stablecoins pillars instead.
